summary:
Female figurines interpreted as representations of a mother goddess or female divine principle have been found at numerous Neolithic sites across Anatolia and the broader Near East, including in abundance at [[event:11|Çatalhöyük]] and related communities dating from approximately 7000 to 6000 BCE. These figurines — typically seated, full-bodied female forms emphasizing fertility attributes — have attracted considerable scholarly debate about their meaning, function, and relationship to later goddess traditions in the ancient Near East.

The interpretation of these figurines as evidence for a widespread Neolithic goddess religion or mother goddess cult was popularized by scholars such as Marija Gimbutas, who proposed an Old European religion centered on a female creative principle before being disrupted by Bronze Age patriarchal traditions. This interpretation has been substantially criticized and revised by subsequent scholarship, which points out that the figurines vary considerably in form and context, that male and animal figurines are also found, and that many figurines may have served diverse purposes — from ritual objects to toys to markers of household identity — rather than being exclusively religious objects.

Regardless of the interpretive debates, the abundance and recurrence of female figurines at Neolithic Anatolian and Near Eastern sites is an undeniable archaeological fact that reflects a significant emphasis on female form and possibly fertility in the symbolic world of early farming communities. The [[event:1509|Seated Woman of Çatalhöyük]], found in a grain bin and interpreted as a goddess seated on a throne flanked by leopards, is one of the most celebrated of these objects.
